children.name: Lemur
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the ring-tailed lemur, arguably the most recognisable of all lemurs. Ring-tailed lemurs are unusual among strepsirrhines in being highly terrestrial and living in female-dominated groups. Their boldly banded tails are held aloft during social encounters.

children.name: Eulemur
children.description: The brown and collared lemurs form the largest genus of Lemuridae, with 13 species distributed across Madagascar's forests from humid east to dry west and introduced to the Comoros. Many show striking sexual dichromatism, with males and females bearing conspicuously different coat colours. The former wide-ranging brown lemur (E. fulvus) has been split into multiple species through molecular and morphological studies.

children.name: Eulemur fulvus
children.description: The nominate brown lemur, now restricted (after splitting) to northwestern Madagascar and introduced populations on Mayotte and Réunion. A generalist frugivore of dry and humid forests, one of the most adaptable Malagasy primates. Lives in mixed-sex groups and shows crepuscular to cathemeral activity patterns.

children.name: Eulemur albifrons
children.description: Endemic to the humid rainforests of northeastern Madagascar, the white-fronted brown lemur is one of several species split from the formerly wide-ranging Eulemur fulvus complex. Males are grey-brown with a distinctive white forehead and cheek fur; females are reddish-brown with an orange-tinted face — one of the most pronounced sexual colour differences in the genus. Cathemeral, meaning active at variable times of both day and night. Critically Endangered from habitat loss.

children.name: Eulemur sanfordi
children.description: A northern Malagasy lemur restricted to the dry deciduous forests of the Ankarana, Analamera, and Montagne d'Ambre regions. Males have cream-white ear tufts and cheek ruffs against a grey-brown coat; females are reddish-brown. Cathemeral activity allows foraging in both daylight and at night, an unusual flexibility among primates. Endangered; its limited range makes it highly vulnerable to deforestation. Named after Leonard C. Sanford, the American naturalist who funded early lemur expeditions.

children.name: Eulemur rufus
children.description: A western Malagasy lemur inhabiting dry deciduous forests along the west coast from the Betsiboka River south to the Fiherenana River. Males are warm reddish-brown with a pale face; females are more greyish. Like other Eulemur it is cathemeral, shifting activity patterns seasonally. The rufous brown lemur was long treated as a subspecies of the common brown lemur and was only fully elevated to species status with the application of molecular phylogenetics in the 2000s. Vulnerable; western Madagascar's dry forests are among the most threatened on the island.

children.name: Eulemur rufifrons
children.description: Distributed in two disjunct populations in both the dry western forests and the humid southeastern rainforests of Madagascar, making it one of the most ecologically flexible brown lemurs. Males have a grey-brown body with a rufous-red forehead; females are reddish-brown throughout. In Ranomafana National Park it has been the subject of decades of field research. Another product of the splitting of the former E. fulvus complex. Vulnerable from forest clearance and fragmentation.

children.name: Eulemur cinereiceps
children.description: One of the rarest and least-known lemurs, restricted to a small area of humid coastal rainforest in the Fort Dauphin region of southeastern Madagascar — a habitat already heavily fragmented and degraded. Males have a grey head and reddish-brown body; females are reddish-brown with a paler face. Also known as the white-collared lemur or white-collared brown lemur. Critically Endangered with one of the most restricted ranges of any Eulemur species. Described as a separate species relatively recently following molecular and morphological reassessment of the southeastern lemur fauna.

children.name: Eulemur mayottensis
children.description: A lemur endemic to Mayotte (Mahoré), the French territory in the Comoro Islands, introduced from Madagascar long before European contact — possibly by Malagasy settlers over a thousand years ago. Genetically and morphologically distinct from all Malagasy Eulemur species, probably derived from an early introduction of E. fulvus or a related species that has since diverged in isolation. Males and females are similar in colouration, brownish-grey with whitish cheek patches. The Mayotte lemur was formally described as a distinct species in 2017 based on morphological and molecular evidence. The Comoros population has no wild conspecifics, making conservation of the Mayotte population uniquely important.

children.name: Varecia
children.description: Ruffed lemurs are the largest of the true lemurs, with a distinctive ruff of long fur around the neck and face. They are unusual primates in building nests for their offspring and in their dispersed fission-fusion social system. Critical seed dispersers for large-fruited trees in Madagascar's rainforests.

children.name: Hapalemur
children.description: Bamboo lemurs are the only primates to specialise almost exclusively on bamboo — including the highly toxic giant bamboo, which they detoxify through physiological adaptations. Five species are endemic to Madagascar and critically threatened by bamboo forest loss.

children.name: Hapalemur alaotrensis
children.description: The most range-restricted of all lemurs, confined entirely to the papyrus and reed beds surrounding Lac Alaotra in central-eastern Madagascar — the island's largest lake. Also known as the bandro, it is the only primate in the world specialised for life in marshland, swimming between reed islands and feeding on reed shoots, sedges and aquatic vegetation. Critically Endangered with a total population estimated at under 3,500 individuals; threatened by the drainage of Lac Alaotra's wetlands for rice cultivation, hunting, and capture for the pet trade. A symbol of the Duke of Edinburgh's Award and the focus of intensive conservation efforts by Durrell Wildlife Conservation Trust, which has established ex-situ breeding colonies.

children.name: Hapalemur meridionalis
children.description: A small bamboo lemur of the humid forests and bamboo thickets of southeastern Madagascar, from Fort Dauphin north to the Ranomafana area. Long treated as a subspecies of the grey bamboo lemur, it was elevated to full species status based on molecular and morphological evidence showing consistent differentiation from northern populations. Like other Hapalemur it feeds primarily on bamboo shoots and leaves, tolerating the cyanide compounds in certain bamboo species that would be lethal to most mammals. Endangered from the severe deforestation of southeastern Madagascar's coastal rainforests.

children.name: Prolemur
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the greater bamboo lemur, the largest of the bamboo specialists. Once thought extinct, rediscovered in 1986. Critically endangered with fewer than 100–200 individuals remaining in fragmented patches of southeastern Madagascar.
